prompt: Name one commonly-praised idea, trend, or practice in modern knowledge work that you think is overrated, and explain why.
response:
One commonly praised idea in modern knowledge work that I think is overrated is the concept of daily standup meetings (also known as scrum meetings). 

The idea behind daily standups is to have a short 15-minute meeting each morning where everyone on the team shares what they worked on yesterday, what they plan to work on today, and if they have any blockers. The goal is to improve coordination and keep everyone informed.

However, I don't believe the benefits justify the time they consume on a daily basis. Many teams end up going over the allotted 15 minutes as people share unnecessary details. The constant interrupting of people's work flow can be counterproductive to making progress, especially for introverted or deep-focused workers.

Most importantly, the information shared in a daily standup has better alternatives - status updates and issues are better communicated in asynchronous channels like team chat or a project management tool. That way people can share updates when they have them, without the rigid daily schedule and time sink.

So while standups have some merits in theory, I believe the drawbacks outweigh the benefits, especially considering there are superior alternatives for team coordination in the modern asynchronous work environment. Team leads should use discretion on their usage and consider if they bring genuine benefit to their unique team situation.
